citations.desc: Attending veterinarian and adequate veterinary care (dealers and exhibitors).
citations.narrative:
The facility is not using appropriate methods to diagnose and treat diseases and injuries for their adult female emu.
The inspectors observed an adult female emu in an outdoor enclosure with two medical concerns.
** She had an area of complete feather loss on her back approximately 6 inches in length. Short pin feathers (new feather
growth) were visible. The skin was pink.
** The emu also had a swelling on her left knee about 4 inches in diameter. The swelling appeared firm and the same
color and texture as her skin (black).
According to facility representatives, the feather loss has been present for months and the swelling on the leg was
observed about a week ago; the facility did not seek veterinary assessment or treatment for the conditions.
Failing to seek timely veterinary assessment and treatment is preventing the attending veterinarian from ensuring timely
and adequate veterinary care for this animal.
This animal must be evaluated by a veterinarian for treatment and the facility must use appropriate methods to prevent,
control, diagnose, and treat diseases and injuries, and ensure the availability of emergency, weekend, and holiday care.
Correction date: March 31, 2026.

The food receptacles used to take feed to several animals housed outdoors (pigs, muntjac, sheep, goats, and bears) were
not clean.
There was a thick layer of brown, odorous material on the bottom and the sides of the buckets. A representative stated it
had been at least 10 days since the receptacles were cleaned.
Unsanitary food receptacles can contaminate the food and cause illnesses in the animals.
Food receptacles shall be kept clean and sanitary at all times.
Correction date: March 31, 2026.
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with a facility representative.
Additional Inspectors:
SCOTT WELCH, VETERINARY MEDICAL OFFICER n

narrative:
2.40(b)(2) 
   
Attending veterinarian and adequate veterinary care (dealers and exhibitors). 
The facility is not using appropriate methods to diagnose and treat diseases and injuries for their adult female emu. 
 
The inspectors observed an adult female emu in an outdoor enclosure with two medical concerns. 
** She had an area of complete feather loss on her back approximately 6 inches in length. Short pin feathers (new feather 
growth) were visible. The skin was pink. 
** The emu also had a swelling on her left knee about 4 inches in diameter. The swelling appeared firm and the same 
color and texture as her skin (black).  
 
According to facility representatives, the feather loss has been present for months and the swelling on the leg was 
observed about a week ago; the facility did not seek veterinary assessment or treatment for the conditions. 
 
Failing to seek timely veterinary assessment and treatment is preventing the attending veterinarian from ensuring timely 
and adequate veterinary care for this animal. 
 
This animal must be evaluated by a veterinarian for treatment and the facility must use appropriate methods to prevent, 
control, diagnose, and treat diseases and injuries, and ensure the availability of emergency, weekend, and holiday care. 
 
Correction date: March 31, 2026. 
3.129(b) 
   
Feeding. 
The food receptacles used to take feed to several animals housed outdoors (pigs, muntjac, sheep, goats, and bears) were 
not clean.  
 
There was a thick layer of brown, odorous material on the bottom and the sides of the buckets. A representative stated it 
had been at least 10 days since the receptacles were cleaned.  
 
Unsanitary food receptacles can contaminate the food and cause illnesses in the animals.

Food receptacles shall be kept clean and sanitary at all times. 
 
Correction date: March 31, 2026. 
 
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with a facility representative. 
 
Additional Inspectors: 
SCOTT WELCH, VETERINARY MEDICAL OFFICER n
